metadata.title: Guideline on clinical evaluation of medicinal products used in weight management
metadata.document_type: Finalized Guideline (adopted by CHMP 23 June 2016; in effect 1 January 2017)
metadata.replaces: CPMP/EWP/281/96 Rev.1 — Guideline on clinical investigation of medicinal products used in weight control
metadata.deduplication_note: Draft version (EMA/CHMP/311805/2014 Draft, 2014-06-26) was previously extracted to ema-draft-guideline-weight-control.json (25 rules). This finalized version (2016-06-23) is NEWER and SUPERSEDES the draft extraction. Extracted fresh per protocol.

rules.rule_text: Reduction of body weight must be the primary efficacy endpoint in pivotal clinical studies for weight management medicinal products. The key scientific question must specify how treatment effect will be measured in light of post-randomisation events (withdrawals, loss-to-follow-up).
rules.verbatim_quote: To document the effect of a product intended for weight management, reduction of body weight should be the primary efficacy endpoint in the pivotal clinical studies. It is important to be precise with respect to the trial objectives. In particular, during the course of follow-up, a relatively high proportion of patients will withdraw from randomised treatment or be lost-to-follow-up. Documenting the key scientific question of interest should include specification of the manner in which the measure of treatment effect will be made in light of these post-randomisation events.

rules.rule_text: Placebo-corrected weight loss must be documented both as absolute (kg) and relative (%) values compared to baseline, and as responder analyses. A statistically significant, placebo-corrected weight loss of at least 5% of baseline weight after 12 months of treatment is a valid primary efficacy criterion.
rules.verbatim_quote: Placebo-corrected measurements of weight loss should be documented both as absolute and relative weight loss (kg and percentage) compared to body weight at randomisation and as responder analyses. Demonstration of a statistically significant, placebo-corrected weight loss of at least 5% of baseline weight after 12 months of treatment is a valid primary efficacy criterion.
rules.exceptions: Unless otherwise justified and agreed with regulators.

rules.rule_text: Responder definitions must include patients with at least 5% and at least 10% weight loss at the end of a 12-month period. Patients who discontinue from the trial before 12 months must be regarded as non-responders.
rules.verbatim_quote: Responder definitions should include patients with at least 5 and 10% weight loss at the end of a 12-month period. Patients who discontinue from the trial before 12 months should be regarded as non-responders.
rules.exceptions: Alternative analyses based on responder definitions that consider other types of post-randomisation events may also be provided.

rules.rule_text: The predictive value of weight loss after short-term treatment (e.g. 12 weeks on target dose) with respect to long-term efficacy must be documented to identify a population with expected long-term benefit and to include potential stopping rules for non-responders in the product label.
rules.verbatim_quote: The predictive value of weight loss after short-term treatment (e.g. 12 weeks treatment on target treatment dose) with respect to long-term efficacy should be documented, in order to better identify a population with expected long-term benefit and include potential 'stopping rules' for non-responders in the product label.

rules.rule_text: Central adiposity (waist circumference or waist-to-hip ratio) must be documented as a secondary endpoint. Body composition measurements using validated methods (DEXA, MRI or CT) should preferably be included as secondary endpoints to demonstrate that weight loss is associated with appropriate loss of body fat (distinct from muscle or body water).
rules.source_section: Section 4.2.1 — Body weight related secondary endpoints
rules.verbatim_quote: Measurements of central adiposity (e.g. waist circumference or waist to hip ratio) should be documented as secondary endpoints. Measurements using accepted and validated methods (i.e. DEXA, magnetic resonance imaging or computer tomography) should preferably be included as secondary endpoints to demonstrate that weight loss is associated with appropriate loss of body fat (as distinct from muscle or body water).

rules.rule_text: A new agent intended for weight management must show a neutral or beneficial effect on parameters associated with cardiovascular risk. The effect on CV risk factors (blood glucose, blood pressure, heart rate, lipid levels) must be documented and may be presented as responder analyses.
rules.source_section: Section 4.2.2 — Effect on cardiovascular risk factors and cardiovascular morbidity/mortality
rules.verbatim_quote: A new agent intended for weight management is expected to show a neutral or beneficial effect on parameters associated with cardiovascular risk.
rules.exceptions: No requirement to demonstrate direct positive effect on CV morbidity/mortality prior to licensing unless specific claims are made.

rules.rule_text: Any claim of a reduction of cardiovascular morbidity and/or mortality must be supported by well-designed clinical trials enrolling patients with overweight and/or obesity representative of the target population with respect to grade of overweight/obesity and baseline cardiovascular risk.
rules.source_section: Section 4.2.2 — Effect on cardiovascular risk factors and cardiovascular morbidity/mortality
rules.verbatim_quote: Any claim of a reduction of cardiovascular morbidity and/or mortality will need to be supported by well-designed clinical trials enrolling patients with overweight and/or obesity representative of the target population with respect to grade of overweight and obesity and baseline cardiovascular risk.

rules.rule_text: Eligible patients for weight management trials are those for whom at least one trial of an appropriate weight-reducing diet has proven to be insufficient. Obesity must be diagnosed based on BMI ≥30 kg/m2; for patients with weight-related risk factors, a lower BMI (≥27 kg/m2) could be considered.
rules.verbatim_quote: Eligible patients are those for whom at least one trial of an appropriate weight-reducing diet has proven to be insufficient. Obesity should be diagnosed on the basis of a body mass index (BMI) of 30 kg/m2 or more in both males and females. For patients with weight related risk factors, a lower BMI at baseline (e.g. ≥27 kg/m2) could be considered.

rules.rule_text: Development programs must always include relevant samples of patients with class II obesity (BMI ≥35 kg/m2) and class III obesity (BMI ≥40 kg/m2) to describe the magnitude of the weight-lowering effect in these subgroups.
rules.verbatim_quote: Considering that the risk of morbidity and mortality as well as other complications increases with increasing BMI, development programs should always include relevant samples of patients with class II (BMI ≥ 35 kg/m2) and class III obesity (BMI ≥ 40 kg/m2) in order to describe the magnitude of the weight lowering effect in these subgroups.

rules.rule_text: A relevant proportion of patients with coexisting cardiovascular risk factors must be enrolled into studies to represent the expected target population. The weight-lowering effect and effect on glycaemic parameters must preferably be documented separately in patients with type 2 diabetes.
rules.verbatim_quote: A relevant proportion of patients with overweight and/or obesity enrolled into studies should have coexisting cardiovascular risk factors in order to represent the expected target population. The weight lowering effect as well as effect on glycaemic parameters should preferably be documented separately in patients with type 2 diabetes.

rules.rule_text: Confirmatory phase III trials must be randomised, placebo-controlled, and double-blind. Minimum duration of the majority of confirmatory trials is 12 months to fully document the effect on weight development and obesity-related comorbidities.
rules.verbatim_quote: Confirmatory phase III trials should be randomised, placebo controlled and double blind. Since weight management can be achieved by diet, exercise and behaviour modification alone, the use of a placebo group is necessary to clearly show that the study drug on top of appropriate non-pharmacological interventions is more effective than the same non-pharmacological interventions alone. At least 12 month duration of the majority of the confirmatory trials is recommended to fully document the effect on weight development and obesity related comorbidities.
rules.exceptions: Active-controlled trial designs may be relevant in addition to placebo-controlled trials as new weight management drugs become available in the EU.

rules.rule_text: Before randomisation, all patients must be given similar instructions, advice and encouragement with regard to diet and behaviour modification. In long-term studies, such instruction must be reinforced at frequent intervals.
rules.verbatim_quote: Before randomisation, all patients should be given similar instructions, advice and encouragement with regard to diet and behaviour modification. In long-term studies, such instruction and advice should be reinforced at frequent intervals.

rules.rule_text: Pharmacokinetic studies must consider physiological changes associated with obesity and their effects on distribution, protein binding, metabolism, and renal excretion of drugs. Relevant drug interactions (e.g., with antihypertensives, glucose-lowering and lipid-modifying agents) must be considered and investigated if relevant.
rules.verbatim_quote: Pharmacokinetic studies should be performed to characterize the disposition of the drug. Physiological changes associated with obesity and their effects on the distribution, protein binding, metabolism and renal excretion of drugs should be considered and investigated if considered relevant. Depending on the drug and its mode of action, relevant interactions (with for example antihypertensives, glucose lowering and lipid modifying agents) should be considered and investigated.

rules.rule_text: Studies must be designed to allow identification and characterisation of clinically important subgroups (e.g., BMI class II/III). A relevant number of patients from EU countries or countries with similar lifestyle and obesity interventions should be included. Data for various age groups (including elderly) must be presented to assess consistency of treatment effect and safety profile.
rules.verbatim_quote: Studies should be designed to allow the applicant to identify and characterise any clinically important sub-groups that respond to the treatment to a greater or lesser extent... With respect to the elderly, data should be presented for various age groups to assess the consistency of the treatment effect and safety profile in these patients in comparison with younger patient populations.
rules.exceptions: Any limitation of the data set in respect to elderly will be reflected in the product information.

rules.rule_text: A comprehensive plan for detection and evaluation of potential adverse events, including justification of study size and duration with respect to detecting safety signals, must be prospectively designed early in clinical development — optimally by the time of Phase II studies.
rules.verbatim_quote: In the clinical studies, an overall plan for the detection and evaluation of potential adverse events, including justification of the size and duration of the studies with respect to the possibility of detecting safety signals, should be prospectively designed early during the clinical development, optimally by the time of phase II studies.

rules.rule_text: Subjects with a history of mild to moderate depression and those using antidepressive treatment must not be excluded from weight management trials (to reflect clinical practice). If there are any indications of neuropsychiatric safety issues, a prospective assessment of psychiatric, neurostimulant or cognitive adverse events must be performed.
rules.verbatim_quote: To reflect clinical practice, subjects with a history of mild to moderate depression and those using anti-depressive treatment should not be excluded from the trials. Some weight management drugs (e.g., those with a central mechanism of action) could have the potential to cause serious neuropsychiatric adverse events. If there are any indications of neuropsychiatric safety issues from mechanistic, non-clinical, early clinical or marketed drug data, then a prospective assessment of psychiatric, neurostimulant or cognitive adverse events (e.g. depression and suicidality, agitation, anxiety, insomnia, psychotic reactions, attention disturbances) should be performed.

rules.rule_text: The potential for abuse/dependence and withdrawal effects must be justified for each specific weight management drug. If there are any indications from mechanistic, nonclinical, early clinical or marketed data, prospective assessment of abuse potential, dependence, and/or withdrawal effects must be performed. Trials must have follow-up of sufficient period to assess potential withdrawal effects.
rules.source_section: Section 7.3 — Abuse potential and withdrawal effects
rules.verbatim_quote: The potential for abuse/dependence and withdrawal effects needs to be justified for each specific drug. If there are any indications from mechanistic, non-clinical, early clinical or marketed data, then prospective assessment of abuse potential, dependence, and/or withdrawal effects should be performed. It is essential that trials have follow up examinations of a sufficient period to assess any potential withdrawal effects.

rules.rule_text: The drug development programme must adequately characterize the cardiovascular safety profile enabling evaluation in the marketing authorisation application. This requirement is particularly important for products with a new mechanism of action or products belonging to a drug class for which CV safety is not yet established or has been questioned.
rules.verbatim_quote: It is expected that the drug development programme, containing all relevant clinical and non-clinical data, adequately characterizes the cardiovascular safety profile enabling an evaluation of the cardiovascular safety in the marketing authorisation application. This refers in particular to products with a new mechanism of action or products belonging to a drug class for which the cardiovascular safety profile is not yet established or questioned.

rules.rule_text: For weight management drugs acting as 5-HT2B receptor agonists (centrally acting anorexigenics), repeated echocardiography examinations (e.g., at baseline and every 6 months) must be included in a relevant proportion of study participants to assess valvulopathy risk. Pulmonary arterial hypertension risk must also be addressed in the development program.
rules.verbatim_quote: Available data support that the mechanism of valvulopathy is attributed to specific agonism for the 5-HT2B receptor (Rothman et al.) and therefore agents stimulating this receptor should be carefully assessed with respect to risk of this condition. This evaluation should include repeated echocardiography examinations (e.g. at baseline and every 6 months) in a relevant proportion of study participants. Use of certain centrally acting anorexigenics has also been associated with an increased risk of pulmonary arterial hypertension. This should also be taken into account in the development program.
rules.exceptions: Applies specifically to agents stimulating the 5-HT2B receptor.

rules.rule_text: Pharmacological (drug) options for weight management are only considered as adjuncts to dietary measures and physical exercise. Pharmacological options are not recommended until at least one trial of an appropriate weight-reducing diet has proved insufficient.
rules.verbatim_quote: Pharmacological options are not recommended until at least one trial of an appropriate weight-reducing diet has proved insufficient, i.e. adequate initial weight loss was not achieved or the individual, despite continuing dietary advice, could not maintain an initial weight loss. Pharmacological options are only considered as an adjunct to dietary measures and physical exercise.
